name: The Overton Window
summary: A model for understanding which policies are politically acceptable at any given time — and how political movements shift the boundaries of what is considered mainstream.
whenToUseIt: When assessing whether a policy proposal is viable, when planning a long-term campaign to shift public opinion, or when understanding why certain ideas are dismissed as 'radical' despite having evidence behind them.
steps: Identify the policy spectrum for your issue (from most restrictive to most permissive)
Map where current public opinion and political consensus sit on that spectrum
Identify the boundaries of the Overton Window — what is considered 'acceptable' vs 'radical'
Determine whether your goal is to work within the window or shift it
If shifting: introduce the idea through thought leaders, media, and advocacy before formal policy proposals
Track movement over time — the window shifts through sustained effort, not single events
example: Same-sex marriage in Australia. In the 1990s, it was outside the Overton Window — considered radical by most. Through sustained advocacy, media representation, international examples, and shifting generational attitudes, it moved from 'radical' to 'acceptable' to 'popular.' By the 2017 postal survey, 61.6% voted Yes. The policy window had shifted so far that even conservative politicians supported the change. This shift took two decades of deliberate political work.
